subsystem: Vim editor — the 'include search' / define-search engine. This is find_pattern_in_path() from Vim's search.c, the routine behind commands like [I, [d, :isearch, gd-style include scanning, insert-mode 'include' completion (i_CTRL-X_CTRL-I), and :psearch. Given a search pattern it walks the current buffer line-by-line, follows #include-style directives to other files (bounded by the 'include' option regexp), and reports/expands/jumps to matches, maintaining a stack of opened included files.
provenance: Identified from unmistakable Vim internals: the char_u/linenr_T/colnr_T/regmatch_T/win_T typedefs, the curbuf/curwin globals, b_fname/b_ffname/b_ml.ml_line_count/b_p_inc/b_p_def buffer members, Vim allocator wrappers (alloc, lalloc_clear, ALLOC_MULT, vim_free, VIM_CLEAR), ml_get()/vim_fgets()/vim_regcomp()/vim_regexec(), option globals p_inc/p_def/p_ic/p_verbose, the CHECK_PATH/FIND_DEFINE/ACTION_* enums, IObuff/IOSIZE/LSIZE, and the FEAT_QUICKFIX/FEAT_PROP_POPUP feature macros. The in-code marker '/* sample: primevul_c_test_0082_vuln */' and the file name eval_0000_cwe416.c further tie it to a known Vim CWE-416 sample. The function signature matches vim/vim search.c find_pattern_in_path().
trust_boundary: The primary untrusted inputs are FILE CONTENTS and BUFFER TEXT that the function scans, plus user-configurable option strings. Attacker-controlled data enters through: (1) the text of every scanned line, obtained from ml_get() on the current buffer (line 101/430/642) and via vim_fgets() from opened included files (line 432/616) — a malicious file the user opens or an included file reachable via 'path' supplies these bytes; (2) new_fname / include target file names derived by regexp match from that same untrusted text (line 113-119), which are then fed to mch_fopen() and getfile() — i.e. opening files whose names come from document content; (3) the 'include' and 'define' option regexps (b_p_inc/p_inc, b_p_def/p_def) that drive vim_regcomp on line 67/75/83. The search pattern (ptr,len) and control flags come from the invoking Vim command. The deeper, structural hazard is LIFETIME: line/ptr/startp/curr_fname all point into memory owned by Vim's buffer/memline subsystem, and this routine performs operations (ml_get of other lines, getfile(), win_split(), prepare_tagpreview()) that can run autocommands or reallocate that memory while those pointers are still held — a classic editor use-after-free surface.

what_the_code_does.code:
find_pattern_in_path(
    char_u	*ptr,		// pointer to search pattern
    int		dir UNUSED,	// direction of expansion
    int		len,		// length of search pattern
    int		whole,		// match whole words only
    int		skip_comments,	// don't match inside comments
    int		type,		// Type of search; are we looking for a type?
				// a macro?
    long	count,
    int		action,		// What to do when we find it
    linenr_T	start_lnum,	// first line to start searching
    linenr_T	end_lnum)	// last line for searching
what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the function signature. ptr/len describe the search pattern as a pointer plus an int length; importantly ptr is NOT necessarily NUL-terminated at len — len is the authoritative bound, and ptr typically points into live buffer text (e.g. the word under the cursor). dir is the completion direction (UNUSED unless completion is active). whole requests whole-word matching, skip_comments suppresses matches inside comments, and type selects the mode (CHECK_PATH, FIND_DEFINE, or plain pattern). count says which match to stop on, action selects the behavior when a match is found (ACTION_SHOW, ACTION_SHOW_ALL, ACTION_EXPAND, ACTION_SPLIT, etc.), and start_lnum/end_lnum bound the line range in the current buffer. Because len is a signed int supplied by the caller, its sign and magnitude are a contract the body must respect.

Local state. files is a heap array of SearchedFile records forming a stack of currently-open included files; bigger is the reallocation target when the stack grows; max_path_depth starts at 50 and doubles when exhausted. curr_fname is seeded to curbuf->b_fname — a pointer OWNED by the current buffer object, not a private copy. lnum/depth/old_files/depth_displayed track position and the file stack. file_line will be a reusable line buffer for reading included files; line points at the text currently being scanned; p/startp are cursors into that text; save_char temporarily holds a byte overwritten with NUL for display. regmatch/incl_regmatch/def_regmatch hold compiled regexps and their startp/endp match pointers, which point back into 'line'. matched/found/did_show are flags. The key observation for later: curr_fname, line, startp and p are all borrowed pointers into buffer/regex-managed memory whose lifetime this function does not control.

The three regprog fields are nulled so the cleanup at the end can unconditionally vim_regfree() them. file_line is allocated with a fixed LSIZE capacity — this is the destination buffer that vim_fgets() will later fill from included files, so all reads from included files are bounded to LSIZE by contract. On allocation failure it returns immediately (note: before 'files' is allocated, so nothing to free yet).

Unless we are in CHECK_PATH/FIND_DEFINE or start-of-line completion, the raw pattern is turned into a regexp. pat is sized alloc(len + 5) and filled by sprintf using either "\<%.*s\>" (whole-word: adds \< and \> word anchors) or "%.*s". The %.*s precision caps output at len characters of ptr, so with whole=TRUE the write is 2 (\<) + up to len + 2 (\>) + 1 (NUL) = len+5 bytes, exactly the allocation — the sizing is deliberately tight and, for a well-formed non-negative len, correct. The compiled regmatch.regprog is used later; pat is freed immediately after compilation. ignorecase()/magic_isset() set case-folding and magic mode.

The 'include' option regexp (buffer-local b_p_inc or global p_inc) is compiled into incl_regmatch — this is what recognizes #include-style lines and, via its match bounds, extracts the target file name. When searching for defines, the 'define' option regexp is compiled into def_regmatch. Case is not ignored for these structural patterns. Both bail to fpip_end on compile failure. These option strings are user/project configurable (e.g. via modeline-influenced settings) and drive which text is treated as a file reference.

The include-file stack is allocated and zeroed for max_path_depth (50) entries; old_files marks the boundary between the active stack (grown from index 0 upward) and the recycle region (grown from the top downward). depth = -1 means we are currently in the original buffer, not an included file. The search range is clamped so end_lnum never exceeds the buffer's line count and at least one line is scanned. line = ml_get(lnum) fetches the first line's text. CRITICAL: ml_get returns a pointer directly into the buffer's memline storage; this pointer is only valid until the next call that fetches a different line or otherwise mutates the buffer. Everything downstream that dereferences 'line' depends on that pointer still being live.

The main scan loop begins. If an 'include' regexp is set and matches the current line, this line references another file. p_fname is the base name used to resolve relative paths (the buffer's full name when scanning the original buffer, otherwise the current included file). The include target's text — either the \zs..\ze region (incl_regmatch.startp[0]/endp[0], pointers back into 'line') or the text after the match — is resolved into an absolute new_fname by find_file_name_in_path()/file_name_in_line(). Thus a file name to be opened is derived entirely from untrusted line content and the user's 'includeexpr'/'path'.

Loop-prevention: before descending into new_fname, the code scans both the active stack (indices 0..depth) and the recycled/visited region (old_files..max_path_depth-1), skipping the gap by jumping i to old_files when it reaches depth+1. If fullpathcmp reports the same file (FPC_SAME), the file was already searched: it may print a '(includes previously listed match)' note, then VIM_CLEAR(new_fname) frees and NULLs it and sets already_searched. This cycle guard is what prevents infinite include recursion (a self-including file).

The CHECK_PATH display block ([I / :checkpath). It prints the include tree with indentation reflecting depth, then displays either the resolved new_fname or, when unresolved, the isolated file-name text from the line itself. Note the temporary NUL-termination trick at lines 229-232: save_char = p[i]; p[i] = NUL; msg_outtrans_attr(p,...); p[i] = save_char; — this WRITES a NUL into the buffer/line memory at offset i and restores it. i is computed by scanning with vim_isfilec (207-208) or STRLEN (215) or the \zs match width (198-199), and can be adjusted by ++i at 224/227. The write at p[i] therefore depends on i and p being consistent with the live extent of 'line'.

If a new file resolved, it is pushed on the stack. When the active region is full (depth+1 == old_files), the stack is doubled: bigger is allocated, the active entries [0..depth] are copied low, the gap is zero-initialized, and the recycle region is copied to the top half; then max_path_depth doubles and the old files array is freed. mch_fopen opens new_fname; on failure new_fname is freed. On success ++depth and, if the grow failed earlier (++depth == old_files), one previously visited file record is deliberately dropped (vim_free(files[old_files].name); ++old_files) — a documented 'forget one visited file' fallback. files[depth].name and curr_fname are both set to new_fname (aliasing: two owners of the same allocation, reconciled by the cleanup logic). A scanning message is shown for ACTION_EXPAND or high verbosity.

When the line is NOT an include line, control enters the define/pattern branch. p starts at 'line'. The search_line: label is a re-entry point used to look for further matches within the same line. If a 'define' regexp is set and matches, p is advanced past the 'define' keyword to the first word character of the defined identifier and define_matched is set. All of this pointer walking is over 'line', which must remain the valid, current buffer/file line.

The actual matching. In the direct-compare path (define match or start-of-line completion), startp = skipwhite(p) and the first len bytes are compared against ptr with MB_STRNICMP/STRNCMP. Then, for whole-word define matches, startp[len] is read to ensure the match doesn't continue into another word character. This is the key over-read surface: both the len-byte compare and the startp[len] probe assume startp points to at least len (or len+1) valid bytes — but startp is skipwhite(p) into an arbitrary line, and len is the pattern length, not the line length. The alternative regexp path sets startp = regmatch.startp[0] and, when skip_comments is on, walks p from line to startp to reject matches inside /* */ or // comments. Every access here is on 'line'.

ACTION_EXPAND (insert-mode include completion). The matched word is delimited with find_word_start/find_word_end and copied into IObuff. When completion is 'adding' and the whole existing word matched, the code fetches the NEXT line — line = ml_get(++lnum) from the buffer, or vim_fgets(line = file_line,...) from an included file — to append the following word (multi-word completion). Note the ordering: STRNCPY(IObuff, aux, i) at line 421 copies from aux (which is startp of the CURRENT line) BEFORE ml_get refetches 'line' at 430, so that copy is from live memory; afterward aux/p are re-pointed to the new line via skipwhite(line). The append copy at 459 is bounded by the check at 457 (if p - aux >= IOSIZE - i clamp). Finally ins_compl_add_infercase records the completion; note it passes curr_fname (a borrowed buffer/name pointer) as the source file. The bounds reasoning here hinges on ins_compl_len() being <= i and IOSIZE headroom.

ACTION_SHOW_ALL ([I) prints every match. It shows the file name (curr_fname) when it changes and calls show_pat_in_path(line, ...) to display the matching line, passing either the buffer lnum (depth==-1) or the included file's fp/lnum. It then marks this file and all ancestors on the stack as matched so the include tree can show which branches contained a hit. curr_fname and line are again borrowed pointers passed straight to display routines.

The jump path: once we reach the requested match (--count <= 0), for ACTION_SHOW it just displays; otherwise it may split the window (win_split), set up a preview window (prepare_tagpreview), and jump to the file with getfile(). These calls are the dangerous ones for lifetime: win_split(), prepare_tagpreview(), and especially getfile() run autocommands (WinNew, BufLeave, BufEnter, BufReadPre/Post, etc.) and switch the current buffer/window. The comment at 565 even acknowledges 'autocommands may have changed the lnum'. After all that, at line 572 the code computes curwin->w_cursor.col = (colnr_T)(startp - line) — pointer arithmetic on startp and line, which point into the ORIGINAL buffer's memline that autocommands may have wiped or reallocated. The invariant that startp and line still refer to live, unmoved memory is exactly what these side-effecting calls can violate.

After handling a match, matched is reset and, in expand mode, the code tries to find further matches later in the same line by advancing startp by one (multibyte) character and jumping back to search_line:. This re-parses the remainder of 'line' — again assuming 'line'/startp are still valid and that startp+mb_ptr2len(startp) stays within the line's bytes.

End of one loop iteration: allow interruption (line_breakcheck / typed-key checks). Then advance to the next line. If we are inside an included file (depth >= 0), vim_fgets reads into file_line; at EOF the file is closed, its record is moved to the recycle region (files[--old_files]), depth is popped, and curr_fname is restored to the parent. When back in the buffer (depth == -1), line = ml_get(lnum) refetches from the buffer. STRLEN(line) then trims trailing CR/LF. Because file_line is a fixed LSIZE buffer that vim_fgets never overruns, the included-file path is bounded; the buffer path re-fetches via ml_get, again handing out a fresh borrowed pointer.

Cleanup. All still-open stack files [0..depth] are fclosed and their names freed; the recycled region names [old_files..max_path_depth-1] are freed; then the files array itself is freed. Note the aliasing consideration from line 285 (files[depth].name = curr_fname = new_fname): each name allocation has exactly one owner among the two regions, so this loop should free each once. Status messages are emitted per type/action. The fpip_end: label frees file_line and vim_regfree()s all three compiled regexps (safe because they were NULL-initialized). This is the single exit for both success and the early error gotos.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Use-after-free / dangling-pointer arithmetic (CWE-416). startp and line are borrowed pointers into the current buffer's memline (line came from ml_get at 101/642; startp is derived from it). Immediately above, in the same branch, the code calls win_split() (536), prepare_tagpreview() (531) and getfile() (548/561) — all of which execute autocommands and switch/close buffers and windows. Autocommands (BufLeave/BufWipeout/BufReadPre etc.) can free or reallocate the original buffer's line storage, leaving 'line' and 'startp' dangling. Line 572 then computes startp - line on freed pointers to set the cursor column; more broadly the loop's model of 'line still points at the scanned text' is destroyed. The implementation must NOT retain ml_get() pointers across any call that can run autocommands; it must re-fetch the line (or capture the column before the jump). This is the CWE-416 pattern the sample is built around: a callback (autocommand) fired mid-processing frees the object under the active use.

Out-of-bounds read (CWE-125). startp points into an arbitrary scanned line (buffer or included file), while len is the length of the SEARCH pattern, not the line. STRNCMP/MB_STRNICMP read up to len bytes of startp; if the current line is shorter than len, the compare reads past the end of the line buffer. Worse, when whole && define_matched, startp[len] is dereferenced to test the following character — an explicit read at offset len with no check that the line has len+1 bytes. An attacker who controls the scanned text (a crafted source file opened/completed against, reached directly or via an include) can make lines shorter than the pattern to force reads past the allocation. This corresponds to the documented find_pattern_in_path over-read family (see vim/vim issue #18195 / the CVE-2022-2571 completion over-read). Invariant: bound every startp access by the actual remaining line length, not by the pattern length.

Latent buffer overflow via signed length (CWE-787 / CWE-120, root cause CWE-190/incorrect calculation). len is a signed int. For a normal non-negative len the sizing alloc(len+5) exactly fits the worst-case sprintf output (\< + len + \> + NUL). But two edge contracts must hold in the implementation: (a) if len is negative, the printf precision '.*' with a negative value is treated as if omitted, so %.*s copies ptr up to its NUL with NO bound — overflowing the tiny/underflowed pat buffer; and (b) alloc(len+5) with a large or negative len can wrap or under-allocate. Because ptr is not guaranteed NUL-terminated at len, correctness depends entirely on the caller passing a sane, non-negative len that truly bounds ptr. Invariant: reject len < 0 (and cap it) before allocating and before using it as a printf precision; prefer vim_snprintf over sprintf.

Out-of-bounds read/write on the temporary NUL-termination (CWE-125/CWE-787). i is computed by scanning file-name characters, then adjusted by ++i when a trailing quote/angle-bracket is detected at p[i] (line 226). After these adjustments the code does save_char = p[i]; p[i] = NUL; — reading and then WRITING at offset i. If the \zs-derived width (i from endp-startp) or the ++i adjustments push p[i] to or past the line's terminating NUL, this writes a NUL one byte beyond the intended text; combined with the \zs case where p may sit near the line start/end, the offset math is fragile. Invariant: p[i] must be guaranteed to lie within the live line buffer before it is read and overwritten; the quote/angle adjustments must be re-validated against the line's actual length.

Resource-management / aliasing fragility (CWE-401 leak or CWE-416/CWE-415 if reconciliation is wrong; low severity). new_fname becomes owned by files[depth].name AND is simultaneously assigned to curr_fname (an alias). The 'forget a visited file' fallback frees files[old_files].name and bumps old_files when the stack could not grow. The cleanup loops at 649-655 free names from both the active region [0..depth] and the recycle region [old_files..max_path_depth-1]; if the depth/old_files bookkeeping ever lets the same name allocation be reachable from both regions (or a moved record retains a stale fp), a name is double-freed or an fp is leaked. Also file names come from untrusted line content and are passed to mch_fopen — opening attacker-named files is itself a mild information-exposure/SSRF-adjacent concern gated by 'path'. Invariant: every name allocation must have exactly one owner across the two regions at all times, and the alias in curr_fname must never be freed independently.

Fixed-buffer index hygiene in IObuff completion assembly (CWE-787, low/latent). i is carried from the earlier STRNCPY(IObuff, aux, i) and then two unconditional IObuff[i++] = ' ' writes are performed BEFORE the length clamp at 457. The code relies on the invariant (comment 'IOSIZE > compl_length, so the STRNCPY works' and 'thus i >= 2') that i is always well below IOSIZE and >= 2 so that IObuff[i-1]/IObuff[i-2] are in-bounds and the ++i writes stay inside IObuff. If ins_compl_len()/word length ever approached IOSIZE, the pre-clamp increments could push i to the edge. Invariant to confirm: i is provably < IOSIZE-2 before the ' ' insertions and >= 2 before reading IObuff[i-2]; the final IObuff[i] = NUL (463) stays in-bounds.

summary: This is Vim's find_pattern_in_path() (search.c), the include/define search and completion engine. The dominant risk is a use-after-free (CWE-416): pointers into buffer-managed memory (line from ml_get, startp, curr_fname) are held across getfile()/win_split()/prepare_tagpreview() calls that run autocommands and can free or move that memory, after which they are still used (e.g. startp - line at line 572). A secondary, well-documented over-read (CWE-125) exists in the len-bounded compare and startp[len] probe at 340-344, plus latent overflow hazards in the sprintf pattern build (signed len) and the p[i] NUL-termination trick. Impact ranges from crashes to potential memory corruption when the user opens or completes against a crafted file.

reviewed_anchors.reason: ptr is a non-NUL-terminated pointer bounded only by len; borrowed from live buffer text — drives the over-read and pattern-build findings.
signed length used as sprintf precision and STRNCMP bound; negative/large values are the root of the pattern-build overflow.
borrowed buffer-owned pointer reused across autocommand-running calls and aliased with files[].name; lifetime/aliasing concern.
holds ml_get() result; central use-after-free surface when buffer is mutated by autocommands.
derived from line/regmatch; dereferenced at len offset (OOB read) and used in startp-line after buffer switch (UAF).
temporary NUL write at computed offset i can land past the line end (OOB write).
heap stack with dual-region ownership and a 'forget one file' fallback; name double-free/leak reconciliation must be verified.
fixed-size read buffer; vim_fgets is bounded by LSIZE, no overflow on the included-file read path.
sizing is exact for non-negative len but negative len makes %.*s unbounded and alloc under-sized — latent overflow.
compiled from user option strings; NULL-checked and vim_regfree'd; no direct memory-safety contract beyond regex engine itself.
constant-bounded allocation (50 entries), NULL-checked; growth handled separately.
returns pointer into memline invalidated by later buffer mutation; source of the UAF.
file name derived from untrusted line text, then fopen'd/getfile'd; ownership aliasing and file-open exposure.
loop guard against include recursion; logic-bounded by i==max_path_depth, no direct memory unsafety.
len-bounded read plus startp[len] probe with no line-length check — OOB read.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: STRNCPY(IObuff, aux, i) / IObuff[i++] / clamp p-aux>=IOSIZE-i (421,447,454,457-460)
reviewed_anchors.reason: fixed IObuff writes rely on i<IOSIZE invariant; pre-clamp ++i writes are latent index hazard.
run autocommands that can free the buffer while line/startp are still held — UAF trigger.
pointer arithmetic on possibly-freed line/startp after the jump — the concrete UAF use site.
reads into fixed LSIZE buffer; bounded, and line trimmed via STRLEN afterward — safe as written.
re-scan of same line advances startp by mb_ptr2len and guards *startp != NUL; bounded, not an unbounded recursion.
frees each region once; correctness depends on the ownership invariant already captured by the files/new_fname finding.
all three regprog fields NULL-initialized at 48-50, so vim_regfree is always safe.

findings.title: Use-after-free: buffer line pointers held across autocommand-running jump (getfile/win_split)
findings.bug_class: Use-after-free (autocommand frees buffer line under active pointer)
findings.invariant: ml_get()-derived pointers (line) and pointers into them (startp) must never be used after any call that can execute autocommands or switch/close buffers; re-fetch the line or capture derived values (e.g. the column) BEFORE win_split()/prepare_tagpreview()/getfile().
findings.failure_mode: When a match is reached with ACTION_SPLIT/ACTION_GOTO/:psearch, win_split(), prepare_tagpreview() and getfile() run user autocommands (BufLeave/BufWipeout/BufReadPre, etc.). An autocommand can wipe or reload the original buffer, freeing/reallocating the memline that 'line' points into. Line 572 then dereferences those dangling pointers via startp - line to set the cursor column, and the loop's 'line' model is corrupted. A crafted buffer/autocmd config yields use-after-free — crash or exploitable memory corruption. This is the CWE-416 contract the sample targets.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm in search.c whether 'line'/'startp' are re-fetched (or the column saved) after getfile()/win_split()/prepare_tagpreview(); check whether autocommands can free curbuf's memline between the ml_get at 101/642 and the use at 572.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read: pattern-length compare and startp[len] probe exceed line length
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (length taken from pattern, not from scanned line)
findings.invariant: Any read of startp must be bounded by the actual number of valid bytes remaining in the current line, not by 'len' (the pattern length); startp[len] must only be read after confirming the line holds at least len+1 bytes.
findings.failure_mode: startp points into an attacker-influenced scanned line (buffer text or an included file's line via vim_fgets). len is the search-pattern length. STRNCMP/MB_STRNICMP read up to len bytes of startp even when the line is shorter, and startp[len] is dereferenced one past the compared window for the whole-word check. A crafted short line near a buffer boundary forces reads past the allocation — information disclosure or crash. Matches the documented find_pattern_in_path completion over-read.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify the line/startp buffer always has >= len (and >= len+1 for the whole-word case) bytes; check vim/vim issue #18195 / CVE-2022-2571 remediation for the same call site.

findings.title: Latent overflow in regexp build: signed len as sprintf precision and allocation size
findings.anchor: pat = alloc(len + 5); sprintf((char *)pat, whole ? "\\<%.*s\\>" : "%.*s", len, ptr);
findings.bug_class: Buffer overflow via negative/oversized signed length (incorrect calculation)
findings.invariant: len must be validated as non-negative and sanely bounded before it is used both as the allocation term (len + 5) and as the printf '.*' precision; ptr must be treated as bounded by len, not NUL.
findings.failure_mode: For a normal non-negative len the alloc(len+5) exactly fits the worst-case output (\< + len chars + \> + NUL). But len is a signed int: a negative len makes '%.*s' behave as if the precision were omitted, so sprintf copies ptr up to its NUL with no bound and overflows the (under-sized/wrapped) pat buffer; a very large len can wrap alloc(len+5). Because ptr is not guaranteed NUL-terminated at len, safety rests entirely on the caller. Impact: heap overflow / corruption if a non-conforming len ever reaches this path.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm all callers pass len >= 0 and that len cannot overflow len+5; prefer vim_snprintf and an explicit len < 0 guard before the alloc/sprintf.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds NUL write in CHECK_PATH file-name isolation
findings.anchor: save_char = p[i]; p[i] = NUL; ... p[i] = save_char;
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read/write at computed offset (temporary NUL-termination)
findings.invariant: The offset i (from the \zs match width or the vim_isfilec scan, then adjusted by ++i on trailing quote/angle) must be proven to index within the live 'line' buffer before p[i] is read and overwritten with NUL.
findings.failure_mode: In the [I / :checkpath display path, i is computed from regexp match width (198-199) or a filename scan, then bumped by ++i when p[i] is a closing quote/angle bracket (226-227). save_char = p[i]; p[i] = NUL writes a NUL at offset i and restores it. With a crafted include line where the \zs region or the trailing-character adjustments push i to/past the line's terminating NUL, this reads and writes one byte beyond the intended text. Impact is limited (transient single-byte write, usually within the line allocation) but is a genuine out-of-bounds hazard on malformed input.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm p[i] is always within the line's allocated length after the ++i adjustments, especially for the \zs-derived i and the p > line branch.

findings.title: File-stack name ownership: dual-region free reconciliation and 'forget one file' fallback
findings.bug_class: Ownership/aliasing error (potential double-free or leak of file-name allocation)
findings.invariant: Each new_fname allocation must have exactly one owner across the active region [0..depth] and recycle region [old_files..max_path_depth-1] at every point; curr_fname is only an alias and must never be freed independently; the grow-failure fallback must not leave a name reachable from both regions.
findings.failure_mode: new_fname is stored in files[depth].name and simultaneously aliased into curr_fname. When the stack could not grow, the fallback frees files[old_files].name and increments old_files. If the depth/old_files bookkeeping ever lets a name allocation remain reachable from both the active and recycle regions (or a stale fp survives a record move), the cleanup loops at 649-655 double-free a name or leak an open fp. Triggered by deep/looping include structures that exhaust and recycle the stack.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace name ownership through the grow, mch_fopen-fail, EOF-pop (617-623) and cleanup (649-655) paths to prove each allocation is freed exactly once and no fp leaks.

audit_checklist: Confirm whether 'line' and 'startp' are re-fetched (or the cursor column captured) BEFORE getfile()/win_split()/prepare_tagpreview() at 531-561; determine if an autocommand can free curbuf's memline before the startp - line use at 572 (primary CWE-416).
Bound every startp/ptr access at 340-344 by the actual line length; verify the line always holds >= len (and >= len+1 for the whole-word startp[len] probe) bytes.
Guard len against negative and oversized values before alloc(len+5) and before using it as the '%.*s' precision at 61-64; migrate sprintf to vim_snprintf.
Prove p[i] stays within the live line for the temporary NUL-termination at 229-232, including the \zs-width and ++i quote/angle adjustments.
Audit file-name ownership across grow (251-268), fopen-fail (273), the 'forget one file' fallback (282-283), EOF-pop (617-623) and final free loops (649-655) to rule out double-free/leak.
Verify IObuff index i stays < IOSIZE across the pre-clamp IObuff[i++] writes at 447/454 and the final IObuff[i] = NUL at 463.
Confirm new_fname derived from untrusted line text (113-119) is safe to hand to mch_fopen()/getfile() with respect to path traversal and unexpected file opens.
